According to the National Minimum Wage Act 2019, s.3(1), every employer shall pay a national minimum wage of not less than N70,000.00 per month to every worker under his establishment, except as otherwise provided under this Act.

However, s.3(1) does not explicitly state what the minimum wage should have been prior to the payment of the final entitlements. But s.3(2) states that the national minimum wage shall be the minimum total amount of money an employer of labour is required to pay the lowest paid worker or employee monthly in his establishment.

This implies that the minimum wage applies to the amount that should have been paid, not the amount that was actually paid. Therefore, the minimum wage law does affect what your last salary should have been.

Regarding the final payment, s.9(1) states that an employer shall pay to the workers a wage not less than the national minimum wage, subject to statutory deductions. This means that the final payment should have been at least the minimum wage, minus any statutory deductions.

However, s.58 of the Nigeria Tax Act 2025 states that the income tax payable on the chargeable income of an individual, other than an individual earning the Minimum Wage, shall be as specified in the Fourth Schedule to this Act.

This implies that the final payment may be subject to income tax, depending on the amount paid and the individual's tax rate. However, without knowing the specific amount paid and the individual's tax rate, it is difficult to determine whether the final payment is taxable.

It is recommended that you consult with a tax professional or a lawyer to determine the specific tax implications of the final payment.

According to [Nigeria Tax Act 2025, s.58], the income tax payable on the chargeable income of an individual, other than an individual earning the Minimum Wage in line with the minimum wage Act, shall be as specified in the Fourth Schedule to this Act.

Since you earn exactly the minimum wage, your income is excluded from the income tax rates specified in the Fourth Schedule of the Act.

Therefore, you do not pay income tax on your minimum wage earnings.

With 5.1 years of continuous employment, the statutory minimum notice your employer must give you (or pay in lieu of) is **one month** [Labour Act 2004, s.11(2)(d)].

- Notice of one week or more must be given in writing [Labour Act 2004, s.11(3)]
- Either party may accept payment in lieu of notice [Labour Act 2004, s.11(6)]
- Payment in lieu is calculated on money wages only, excluding overtime and other allowances [Labour Act 2004, s.11(9)]

According to Section 11 of the Labour Act 2004, the notice period for a contract of employment is as follows:

- 1 day for contracts of 3 months or less
- 1 week for contracts of more than 3 months but less than 2 years
- 2 weeks for contracts of 2 years but less than 5 years
- 1 month for contracts of 5 years or more

Since you've been employed for 18 months, which is more than 3 months but less than 2 years, the notice period is 1 week.

According to Section 11(3) of the Labour Act 2004, any notice for a period of 1 week or more must be in writing.

However, Section 11(3) of the Labour Act 2004 also states that the employer can pay wages in lieu of notice, but only if the contract has terminated. 

Therefore, you are owed 1 week of notice, and your employer can pay you in lieu of notice, but only after the contract has terminated.

receipts.source_text:
11. (1) Either party to a contract of employment may terminate the contract on the expiration of notice given by him to the other party of his intention to do so.

(2) The notice to be given for the purposes of subsection (1) of this section shall be-

(a) one day, where the contract has continued for a period of three months or less;

(b) one week, where the contract has continued for more than three months but less than two years;

(c) two weeks, where the contract has continued for a period of two years but less than five years; and

(d) one month, where the contract has continued for five years or more.

(3) Any notice for a period of one week or more shall be in writing.

(4) The periods of notice specified in subsection (2) of this section exclude the day on which notice is given.

\(5) Nothing in this section affects any right of either party to a contract to treat the contract as terminable without notice by reason of such conduct by the other party as would have enabled him so to treat it before the making of this Act.

(6) Nothing in this section shall prevent either party to a contract from waiving his right to notice on any occasion, or from accepting a payment in lieu of notice.

(7) All wages payable in money shall be paid on or before the expiry of any period of notice.

(8) If an employer gives notice to terminate the contract of employment of a worker who has been continuously employed for three months or more, the employer shall not be liable under this section to make any payment in respect of a period during which the worker is absent from work with the leave of the employer granted at the request of the worker.

(9) In the calculation of .a payment in lieu of notice, only that part of the wages which a worker receives in money, exclusive of overtime and other allowances, shall be taken into account.
